faqs.a: Neither is objectively easier, but they're shaped completely differently. Leopard geckos need a wide, ground-level floor space with minimal climbing decor. Crested geckos need a tall, vertically oriented enclosure with branches and plants to climb, since they live in the trees rather than on the ground.

title: Praying Mantis Ootheca: What to Do If Your Mantis Lays Eggs
seoTitle: Praying Mantis Ootheca: What to Do
excerpt: What a praying mantis ootheca is, whether it can be fertile without a male, how to care for it, and what to do when dozens of nymphs hatch at once.
imageAlt: Praying mantis on a branch next to its foam-like ootheca egg case

faqs.q: Do I need a male mantis for my female to lay an ootheca?
faqs.a: No - an unmated female will often still produce an ootheca on her own schedule, similar to an unfertilized egg from a hen. Whether it's fertile depends on whether mating actually occurred, not on whether an ootheca was produced at all.

faqs.q: How long does it take an ootheca to hatch?
faqs.a: It varies widely by species and temperature, ranging from a few weeks up to several months. Some species require a period of cold diapause (a temperature-triggered dormancy) before they'll hatch at all, so research your specific species before assuming a timeline.

faqs.q: Can I keep the ootheca in the same enclosure as the mother?
faqs.a: Yes, this is generally safe - mantises don't guard or interact with their oothecae the way some other animals tend eggs. Just make sure the ootheca is securely attached to a stable surface and won't be disturbed by regular enclosure maintenance.

faqs.q: What do I do with dozens of newly hatched mantis nymphs?
faqs.a: Separate them into individual containers as quickly as possible. Mantis nymphs are highly cannibalistic toward each other, and a shared enclosure after hatching can result in most of the brood being lost within hours if they aren't split up and fed promptly.

faqs.q: Can an ootheca dry out and fail to hatch?
faqs.a: Yes, insufficient humidity is a common cause of failed hatches. Light misting appropriate to your species' normal humidity range, without soaking the ootheca directly, helps prevent this while it incubates.

title: How Much Does a Rabbit Really Cost?
seoTitle: Rabbit Cost: Full Price Breakdown
excerpt: Rabbits have a reputation as a simple starter pet, but the real cost picture is bigger than the low adoption fee suggests, especially once spay/neuter is factored in.
imageAlt: Rabbit in a fully equipped exercise pen with hay and dishes

faqs.q: What does it cost to set up for a new rabbit?
faqs.a: Most collective initial supplies (exercise pen, litter box, dishes, nail clippers, hay feeder, and bunny-proofing) run $150 to $300, with fuller setups reaching $600.

faqs.q: Why is spay or neuter such a big line item?
faqs.a: It's the cost new owners most often underestimate. Expect roughly $150 to $500 or more, a neuter typically runs $200 to $300, while a spay costs more since it's more invasive. Adoption fees, often just $25 to $100, frequently already include the spay/neuter plus an initial vet check, which can make adopting an already-altered rabbit the cheapest overall path.

faqs.q: What are the ongoing monthly costs for a rabbit?
faqs.a: Hay ($20-40), pellets ($5-15), fresh vegetables ($15-50), and litter ($15-25) typically total $60 to $100 a month. Add routine vet care and a realistic annual total lands around $500 to $800 after the first year.

faqs.q: How much should I set aside for a rabbit vet emergency?
faqs.a: At least $500 to $1,000, kept separate from your routine budget. Rabbits need an exotic or rabbit-savvy vet, hide illness well, and a single serious issue like a GI stasis workup alone can exceed $1,500.

title: Rabbit GI Stasis: The Silent Emergency Every Owner Should Recognize
seoTitle: Rabbit GI Stasis: A Silent Emergency
excerpt: Rabbit GI stasis can turn fatal within hours - the early warning signs, why unlimited hay matters so much, and when it becomes an emergency vet visit.
imageAlt: Healthy rabbit eating a large pile of fresh hay in a clean enclosure

faqs.q: How quickly can GI stasis become fatal?
faqs.a: It can turn life-threatening within 24 to 48 hours of onset. Gas builds up in a gut that's stopped moving, causing severe pain, and the longer it goes untreated the harder it becomes to reverse - this is why any rabbit that stops eating needs same-day veterinary attention, not a wait-and-see approach.

faqs.q: Can GI stasis happen even if my rabbit eats hay every day?
faqs.a: Yes. Hay is the single biggest prevention factor, but it doesn't make a rabbit immune. Stress, dehydration, dental pain, or an unrelated illness can all trigger stasis even in a rabbit with an otherwise excellent diet.

faqs.q: Is GI stasis the same thing as bloat?
faqs.a: They're related but not identical. GI stasis refers to the gut slowing or stopping movement generally, which often leads to gas accumulation. Severe gas buildup (bloat) is one dangerous consequence of stasis, but stasis can also present with reduced motility and minimal gas.

faqs.q: Can I treat GI stasis at home with pineapple juice or gas drops?
faqs.a: No - these are outdated home remedies with no reliable evidence behind them, and relying on them wastes critical time. GI stasis needs veterinary treatment (fluids, pain control, motility medication, and addressing the underlying cause), not a kitchen-cabinet fix.

faqs.q: How can I tell if my rabbit's poop output is abnormal?
faqs.a: Know your rabbit's normal output as a baseline. Watch for noticeably fewer droppings than usual, droppings that are smaller or oddly shaped, a string of droppings connected by fur or debris, or no droppings at all for several hours - any of these warrants immediate attention.

title: How to Handle a Rabbit Without Hurting It
seoTitle: How to Handle a Rabbit Safely
excerpt: Rabbits are more physically fragile than most first-time owners expect. An unsupported or panicked rabbit can genuinely injure itself trying to escape your grip.
imageAlt: Rabbit being carried securely with full body support against a person

faqs.q: Why does rabbit handling need so much support?
faqs.a: A rabbit that struggles against an unsupported hold can fracture its own lumbar spine, the strength of its powerful hind legs works against it in exactly the wrong way if its body isn't fully supported. This is the single most important thing to get right with this species.

faqs.q: What's the correct way to pick up a rabbit?
faqs.a: Support the chest with one hand (positioned just behind the front legs) and the hindquarters with the other, simultaneously. Hold the rabbit close against your body rather than out at arm's length, and never lift by the ears or rely on the scruff alone to support its weight.

faqs.q: Is 'trancing' a rabbit safe or calming?
faqs.a: No. Trancing, flipping a rabbit onto its back, is often mistaken for calming, but research measuring rabbits in this position found significant increases in respiration, heart rate, and stress hormone levels. It's a fear response the animal can't escape, not relaxation.

faqs.q: Why won't my rabbit relax when I pick it up, even though it trusts me?
faqs.a: That's normal. Most rabbits are calmest with all four feet on the ground, and many never come to love being picked up even once they fully trust their owner. Build trust through floor-level interaction instead, sitting at the rabbit's level and letting it choose to approach.

title: Common Rabbit Health Issues and Why You Need the Right Vet
seoTitle: Rabbit Health Issues: Signs & Causes
excerpt: Rabbits need a rabbit-savvy exotic vet, not a standard small-animal clinic. Here's what to watch for, from GI stasis to dental disease, and when it's a true emergency.
imageAlt: Rabbit being examined by an exotic veterinarian

faqs.q: What is the most common rabbit emergency?
faqs.a: GI stasis, where a rabbit's gut slows down or stops entirely, allowing gas to build up. A rabbit that hasn't eaten or passed stool in 8 hours or more is an emergency, not a wait-and-see situation. See our dedicated GI stasis guide for the full picture.

faqs.q: Why do rabbits need dental checkups?
faqs.a: Rabbit teeth grow continuously, as much as 4 to 5 inches a year, and overgrowth or molar spurs cause drooling, dropped food, and weight loss. Treatment often means a sedated trim, and the best prevention is a properly hay-based diet that wears teeth down naturally.

faqs.q: What is flystrike and why is it an emergency?
faqs.a: Flies lay eggs on soiled or damp fur, and the maggots that hatch can kill a rabbit within 24 to 48 hours. It's a particular risk for rabbits kept outdoors or any rabbit with mobility issues or urine-soaked fur that isn't caught and cleaned promptly.

faqs.q: How common is uterine cancer in unspayed female rabbits?
faqs.a: Up to 80% of unspayed does aged 5 and older develop uterine cancer, with risk beginning to climb from around age two. This is one of the strongest arguments for spaying a female rabbit early, well before this becomes a concern.

title: Rabbit Housing: Why the Old Pet-Store Cage Isn't Enough
seoTitle: Rabbit Housing Setup Guide
excerpt: Rabbit housing guidance has shifted substantially. The space rabbits actually need is much larger than what's traditionally been sold as a starter cage.
imageAlt: Rabbit exercise pen setup with litter box, hay feeder, and hides

faqs.q: How much space does a rabbit actually need?
faqs.a: The House Rabbit Society recommends at least 8 square feet of enclosure space combined with at least 24 square feet of exercise space for one to two rabbits, with 5+ hours a day to run and play. A large foldable metal exercise pen, 4x4 feet or bigger, is the realistic standard.

faqs.q: Should a rabbit live indoors or outdoors?
faqs.a: Indoors is strongly the better choice. Indoor rabbits typically live 7 to 10 years, while outdoor rabbits average closer to 2, due to predators, extreme weather, and health problems that go unnoticed without daily close contact.

faqs.q: How do you litter train a rabbit?
faqs.a: Put hay directly in or right over the litter box, since rabbits naturally tend to eliminate while they eat. Use a large, cat-litter-box-style container with paper-based or compressed wood-pellet litter, and avoid clumping cat litter and pine or cedar shavings entirely.

faqs.q: What should make up most of a rabbit's diet?
faqs.a: Hay, about 80 to 85% of the diet, unlimited access, not a side item. Fresh leafy greens come next, and pellets are the smallest piece, about 1/4 cup per 5 pounds of body weight. Fruit and carrots are treats only, not a diet staple.

title: Understanding Reptile Shedding (Ecdysis)
excerpt: Shedding is one of the most misunderstood aspects of reptile care. Here's what's actually happening, what's normal, and when you need to step in.
imageAlt: Healthy leopard gecko during a clean shed with perfect skin coming off

faqs.q: Is it normal for my reptile to stop eating before a shed?
faqs.a: Yes, this is common during the pre-shed (dyscratic) phase, which can last 5 to 14 days. Appetite typically returns to normal once the shed is complete.

faqs.q: How often should a healthy reptile shed?
faqs.a: It varies widely by species, age, and growth rate - juveniles shed far more often than adults since they're growing faster. There's no universal schedule; focus on whether each shed comes off cleanly and completely rather than counting days between them.

faqs.q: Is it safe to help peel off shed skin myself?
faqs.a: Only in limited, low-risk cases like gently removing loosened retained shed from toes after a warm soak. Never attempt to remove retained eye caps on snakes yourself - that requires a veterinarian.

faqs.q: Does low humidity always cause stuck shed?
faqs.a: It's the most common cause, but not the only one. Missing rough surfaces to initiate the peel, nutritional deficiencies, and underlying health issues can all cause dysecdysis even when humidity looks adequate on paper.

faqs.q: How long should a soak be for helping with a stuck shed?
faqs.a: 15 to 20 minutes in shallow, warm water is typical, always supervised. This softens retained shed enough to gently assist its removal without stressing or injuring the animal.

title: Brumation in Pet Snakes: How to Safely Cool Down Ball Pythons and Corn Snakes
seoTitle: Brumation in Pet Snakes Explained
excerpt: What brumation is, why most pet snake keepers can safely skip it, and how to cool a ball python or corn snake properly if you plan to breed.
imageAlt: Ball python coiled in a dim, cool terrarium hide during brumation

faqs.q: Do pet snakes need to brumate if I'm not breeding them?
faqs.a: No. Brumation is primarily relevant for triggering reproductive cycling in breeding programs. A pet-only snake kept at stable, appropriate temperatures year-round doesn't need deliberate brumation, though many snakes naturally show reduced appetite and activity in cooler months regardless.

faqs.q: How is brumation different from hibernation?
faqs.a: Hibernating mammals enter a deep, largely unresponsive state with dramatically reduced metabolic function. Brumating reptiles slow down significantly but remain capable of waking and moving if disturbed, and may surface briefly to drink even during the cooling period.

faqs.q: How much weight loss during brumation is safe?
faqs.a: Most breeders consider up to roughly 10% of body weight an acceptable range over a full brumation cycle, though this varies by individual and species. Weighing the snake before starting and checking periodically during the cooling period is the standard way to monitor this.

faqs.q: Can brumating my snake go wrong?
faqs.a: Yes - cooling an unhealthy, underweight, or parasite-carrying snake can be genuinely dangerous, which is why a vet check and a proper pre-brumation fast are standard practice beforehand. Warning signs to abort include excessive weight loss, respiratory symptoms, or lethargy beyond the expected slowdown.

faqs.q: How long should I fast my snake before starting brumation?
faqs.a: Most keepers stop feeding 1 to 2 weeks before cooling begins, giving the snake enough time to fully digest its last meal. Cooling down with undigested food in the gut risks it rotting internally, since digestion slows dramatically along with everything else.

title: UVB Lighting: Why It Matters More Than You Think
excerpt: Recent research has changed what we know about reptile UVB needs - including species once thought not to need it. Here's the updated picture.
imageAlt: Bearded dragon under a modern T5 UVB light fixture showing proper lighting gradient

faqs.q: Do nocturnal reptiles really need UVB if they're never active in daylight?
faqs.a: Current research says yes, at a low level. Even nocturnal and crepuscular species receive incidental UV exposure at dawn and dusk in the wild, and low-output UVB (Zone 1) is now commonly recommended for species previously thought not to need it at all.

faqs.q: How do I know what Ferguson Zone my reptile needs?
faqs.a: It's based on the species' natural basking behavior in the wild - shade-dwellers and crepuscular species sit in Zone 1 or 2 (low UV), while open baskers like bearded dragons and intense sun-seekers like ackie monitors sit in Zone 3 or 4 (moderate to high UV). Species-specific care guides typically state the appropriate zone directly.

faqs.q: How often do UVB bulbs actually need replacing?
faqs.a: Every 12 months for most linear T5 HO bulbs, even if the visible light still looks bright. UV output degrades well before visible brightness noticeably drops, so relying on how the bulb looks is not a reliable way to judge it.

faqs.q: Can I provide too much UVB?
faqs.a: Yes - without a proper gradient allowing the animal to move toward or away from the light, overexposure is possible and can cause harm. A gradient that lets the reptile self-regulate its own exposure is just as important as the bulb strength itself.

faqs.q: Is a compact UVB bulb as good as a linear T5 HO?
faqs.a: Compact coil and dome UVB bulbs generally produce a less even gradient and can be associated with eye and skin irritation in some cases. Linear T5 HO fixtures from reputable brands are the current gold standard recommended by most reptile lighting researchers.
